response: The internet traces its roots to 1960s U.S. Defense Department research, blossoming with ARPANET’s first 1969 message and the 1983 adoption of TCP/IP that set modern standards. Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 creation of the World Wide Web—built on HTML, URLs, and HTTP—and the 1993 Mosaic browser opened online access to the public. A late-1990s dot-com boom poured billions into web start-ups, many collapsing in 2001, yet survivors such as Amazon and Google thrived; the mid-2000s rise of Facebook, Twitter, YouTube, smartphones (post-2007 iPhone), and AWS-driven cloud computing further reshaped communication and commerce. Today the internet links more than 5 billion people and underpins nearly every facet of global life.
